category_summary:
**Anthropic** [dominated the day with a record](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-c08485c6fdc8) **$65B Series H** at a **$965B valuation**, [run-rate revenue crossing](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-12f511f89ac6) **$47B**, and [the launch of](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-e30f206fb535) **Claude Opus 4.8**.

- **Boris Cherny** detailed **Opus 4.8** as Anthropic's strongest coding model (SWE-bench Pro 64.3→69.2), with [high-effort defaults](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-f4ab2e27273d) and [new dynamic workflows](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-94afe04eb962) in **Claude Code**.
- **Ethan Mollick** shared early-access demos: [one-shot shaders](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-be1739f1a81b), [autonomous RPG building](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-46efae9f1cc5), and [end-to-end academic papers](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-46d9b31a71c2), using **GPT-5.5 Pro** as a reviewer.
- Skeptics pushed back: **Gary Marcus** [amplified an Axios scoop](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-d569c2195e82) on **Polymarket's $500M** accidental Claude spend, [argued demos fail on messy inputs](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-5da071c4cf66), and [declared tokenmaxxing over](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-3edb19d8fc8f).
- **Yann LeCun** [clarified that world models](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-ed54619275dd) trained on diverse data become foundation models, reiterating **JEPA**'s [non-pixel approach](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-248093c65574).
- **Clément Delangue** highlighted a **Hugging Face** breakthrough making [async RL weight sync ~100x cheaper](/?date=2026-05-29&category=social#item-c84d07a22346) on bandwidth.

Sentiment split between excitement over Opus 4.8's agentic capabilities and growing concern about AI economics, valuations, and real-world ROI.

items.title: The HF science team just made async RL weight sync ~100x cheaper on bandwidth, and you don't need a ...
items.content:
The HF science team just made async RL weight sync ~100x cheaper on bandwidth, and you don't need a shared cluster anymore.

The problem: every RL step, the trainer typically has to sync fresh weights to the inference engine. for a 7B in bf16 that's ~14GB. for a frontier 1T fp8 checkpoint, that's ~1TB; in bf16 it would be ~2TB. per sync.

The insight: between two RL steps, ~99% of bf16 weights are bit-identical. at RL learning rates, the optimizer is whispering and bf16 literally cannot hear most of it. the stored bf16 bits don't change.

What they shipped in TRL: only the changed elements get encoded as a sparse safetensors file, dropped into a Hugging Face Bucket, and fetched by vLLM. on Qwen3-0.6B, per-step payload goes from 1.2 GB to 20 to 35 MB. This is exactly what we built Buckets for: S3-like object storage on the Hub, Xet-backed (so even full snapshots only transfer the changed chunks).

The cherry on top: we ran a FULL disaggregated training where:
- the trainer lived on one box
- vLLM ran inside a Hugging Face Space
- the Wordle environment ran in another Space
- weights flowed through one Hub bucket

no shared cluster. no RDMA. no VPN. no NCCL across clouds. just HTTPS and a bucket.

one GPU + a Hugging Face account is now enough to do real disaggregated RL. multi-replica inference fleets across regions become a small devops exercise, not a research project.

Full write-up: https://t.co/CG115IjT0q

Open source RL keeps eating the moat!

items.title:
@anshulkundaje Those are orthogonal concepts. 
- World models trained on highly diverse data become ...
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: LeCun clarifies that world models trained on diverse data become foundation models, and that 'world' refers to predicting system evolution and action-conditioned dynamics needed for planning.
items.reasoning: Substantive conceptual explanation of world models from a leading researcher with very high engagement; strong intellectual value.
items.summary_html: LeCun clarifies that world models trained on diverse data become foundation models, and that 'world' refers to predicting system evolution and action-conditioned dynamics needed for planning.
items.content_html:
@anshulkundaje Those are orthogonal concepts.

  • World models trained on highly diverse data become foundation models: their encoders can be used for a wide variety of downstream tasks.
  • "World" refers to two things: (1) predicting the evolution of a complex system or environment, (2) predicting the evolution of a system under control and its effect on the environment (action-conditioned world model) which is a necessary component of planning.

items.title: 4.8 defaults to high effort, which spends about the same tokens as 4.7's default on coding but perfo...
items.content:
4.8 defaults to high effort, which spends about the same tokens as 4.7's default on coding but performs better.

For hard problems and long-running async work, switch to xhigh. We've raised Claude Code rate limits to cover the extra tokens.

items.title: “The mechanism is always the same in every story I've been covering. The demo works in a controlled ...
items.content: “The mechanism is always the same in every story I've been covering. The demo works in a controlled environment with clean inputs. The deployment fails because real kitchens, real intersections, and real warehouses produce messy inputs the demo never tested. The vendor gets paid through the failure cycle. The buyer eats the cost and quietly retires the product. If a pizza chef can lose $250,000 on a topping robot, the people writing $80 billion capex checks for general purpose AI agents should expect to learn the same lessons at much larger scale”
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus argues that AI demos succeed in clean controlled settings but fail on messy real-world inputs, with vendors profiting through failure cycles, warning that multibillion-dollar AI agent capex will repeat these lessons at scale.
items.reasoning: Sharp, widely-shared critique from a prominent AI skeptic on deployment economics and the demo-to-production gap; strong engagement and a recurring industry theme.
items.summary_html: Gary Marcus argues that AI demos succeed in clean controlled settings but fail on messy real-world inputs, with vendors profiting through failure cycles, warning that multibillion-dollar AI agent capex will repeat these lessons at scale.

The deployment fails because real kitchens, real intersections, and real warehouses produce messy inputs the demo never tested. The vendor gets paid through the failure cycle. The buyer eats the cost and quietly retires the product. If a pizza chef can lose $250,000 on a topping robot, the people writing $80 billion capex checks for general purpose AI agents should expect to learn the same lessons at much larger scale”

items.title: We also shipped dynamic workflows in Claude Code (research preview), for tasks too big for one pass....
items.content:
We also shipped dynamic workflows in Claude Code (research preview), for tasks too big for one pass. Make sure to default to auto mode so Claude isn't stopping for permissions.

It's token-intensive, so save it for your biggest jobs: migrations, refactors, perf optimization, batch bug fixes.

items.title:
We're taking on the hardest problems in the real world 🏗️🚚 🛫⚛️

Today at The AI Now Summit, held at ...
items.content:

items.title:
This #CVPR2026 paper from our research team is trending #1 on @HuggingFace 🤗

Meet LocateAnything: a...
items.content:
This #CVPR2026 paper from our research team is trending #1 on @HuggingFace 🤗

Meet LocateAnything: a vision-language detection model that rethinks bounding box prediction. For AI agents and robots, “seeing” is only useful if a model can pinpoint where something is fast enough to act.

Trained on 138M high-quality samples, LocateAnything decodes bounding boxes in parallel instead of one coordinate at a time, improving localization accuracy while dramatically increasing throughput for visual grounding and detection. 

Project page: https://t.co/O7JMe8tzFM

items.title: This is hard! It involves raymarching repeating gothic architecture (instancing towers across an inf...
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Mollick shares the full shader code (raymarched gothic city, displaced ocean, atmospheric fog, no textures) generated by Opus 4.8 to illustrate its technical coding ability.
items.reasoning: Detailed technical artifact showcasing model capability with good engagement; substantive demonstration.
items.summary_html: Mollick shares the full shader code (raymarched gothic city, displaced ocean, atmospheric fog, no textures) generated by Opus 4.8 to illustrate its technical coding ability.
items.content_html:
This is hard! It involves raymarching repeating gothic architecture (instancing towers across an infinite grid with gothic silhouettes and windows), a displaced ocean surface with believable wave motion, and stormy atmospheric lighting/fog to tie it together... and doing this all with no textures or external assets, just math.

Here is the full code:

/* D R O W N E D C A T H E D R A L C I T Y · mk2 — (classic) */

precision highp float;

uniform vec2 resolution;

uniform vec2 mouse;

uniform float time;

#define CITY_STEPS 90

#define WATER_STEPS 60

#define REFL_STEPS 20

#define VOL_STEPS 18

#define FAR 200.0

#define CELL 8.0

#define CREST_K 2.2 // wave crest sharpness (1 = round sine, higher = choppier)

#define WIN_LIT 0.46 // fraction of windows lit (lower = more lit)

#define BEAM_BRIGHT 0.07 // lighthouse beam intensity

#define BEAM_SPEED 0.50 // beam rotation speed

const vec3 SUN = vec3(-0.548, 0.685, -0.480);

/* ---------- hash / noise ---------- */

float hash21(vec2 p){ p=fract(p*vec2(123.34,345.45)); p+=dot(p,p+34.345); return fract(p.x*p.y); }

float hash11(float n){ return fract(sin(n)*43758.5453123); }

float noise(vec2 p){

vec2 i=floor(p), f=fract(p); f=f*f*(3.0-2.0*f);

return mix(mix(hash21(i),hash21(i+vec2(1,0)),f.x),

mix(hash21(i+vec2(0,1)),hash21(i+vec2(1,1)),f.x),f.y);

}

float fbm(vec2 p){

float v=0.0,a=0.5; mat2 m=mat2(1.6,1.2,-1.2,1.6);

for(int i=0;i<4;i++){ v+=a*noise(p); p=m*p; a*=0.5; } return v;

}

/* ---------- ocean ---------- */

float crest(float s){ float u=0.5+0.5*s; u=pow(u,CREST_K); return u*2.0-1.0; }

float oceanLow(vec2 p){

float t=time, h=0.0;

h += 0.95*crest(sin(dot(p,vec2( 0.16, 0.11))+t*1.10));

h += 0.70*crest(sin(dot(p,vec2(-0.12, 0.21))-t*0.90));

h += 0.50*crest(sin(dot(p,vec2( 0.09,-0.17))+t*0.75));

h += 0.30*crest(sin(dot(p,vec2( 0.23, 0.06))+t*1.35));

return h;

}

float oceanHigh(vec2 p){

float h=oceanLow(p);

h += (fbm(p*0.35+vec2(0.0,time*0.18))-0.5)*0.75;

h += (fbm(p*0.90-vec2(time*0.16,0.0))-0.5)*0.30;

h += (fbm(p*2.10+vec2(time*0.25,0.0))-0.5)*0.12;

return h;

}

/* ---------- sdf ---------- */

float sdBox(vec3 p, vec3 b){ vec3 q=abs(p)-b; return length(max(q,0.0))+min(max(q.x,max(q.y,q.z)),0.0); }

float sdCappedCone(vec3 p, float h, float r1, float r2){

vec2 q=vec2(length(p.xz),p.y);

vec2 k1=vec2(r2,h), k2=vec2(r2-r1,2.0*h);

vec2 ca=vec2(q.x-min(q.x,(q.y<0.0)?r1:r2),abs(q.y)-h);

vec2 cb=q-k1+k2*clamp(dot(k1-q,k2)/dot(k2,k2),0.0,1.0);

float s=(cb.x<0.0&&ca.y<0.0)?-1.0:1.0;

return s*sqrt(min(dot(ca,ca),dot(cb,cb)));

}

float towerSDF(vec3 p){

vec2 cid=floor(p.xz/CELL);

vec2 local=mod(p.xz,CELL)-0.5*CELL;

vec3 q=vec3(local.x,p.y,local.y);

float r0=hash21(cid), r1=hash21(cid+5.7);

float halfW=0.85+r1*0.75;

float botY=-9.0, topY, spireH;

if(r0<0.18){ topY=-5.0; spireH=0.0; }

else { topY=1.0+r0*15.0; spireH=2.0+r1*5.5; }

float cy=0.5*(botY+topY), halfH=0.5*(topY-botY);

float s1=sdBox(q-vec3(0.0,cy,0.0),vec3(halfW, halfH,halfW*0.55));

float s2=sdBox(q-vec3(0.0,cy,0.0),vec3(halfW*0.55, halfH,halfW));

float d=min(s1,s2);

if(spireH>0.0){

d=min(d,sdCappedCone(q-vec3(0.0,topY+spireH*0.5,0.0),spireH*0.5,halfW*1.0,0.04));

float pin=1.0+r1*1.6; // 4 corner pinnacles (abs-mirrored)

vec3 qc=vec3(abs(q.x)-halfW*0.72, q.y-(topY+pin*0.5), abs(q.z)-halfW*0.72);

d=min(d,sdCappedCone(qc,pin*0.5,0.16,0.02));

}

return d;

}

vec3 calcNormal(vec3 p){

vec2 e=vec2(0.01,0.0);

return normalize(vec3(towerSDF(p+e.xyy)-towerSDF(p-e.xyy),

towerSDF(p+e.yxy)-towerSDF(p-e.yxy),

towerSDF(p+e.yyx)-towerSDF(p-e.yyx)));

}

vec3 waterNormal(vec3 p){

float e=0.10, h=oceanHigh(p.xz);

return normalize(vec3(h-oceanHigh(p.xz+vec2(e,0.0)), e, h-oceanHigh(p.xz+vec2(0.0,e))));

}

/* ---------- gothic window: pointed (equilateral) arch + tracery + glow ---------- */

vec3 windowField(vec2 f){ // f in [-0.5,0.5]; returns (pane, glow, glassArea)

float halfW=0.30, bottom=-0.42, sh=0.02, A=0.38, e=0.012;

float c=(A*A-halfW*halfW)/(2.0*halfW), R=halfW+c;

float rx=smoothstep(halfW,halfW-e,abs(f.x));

float ry=smoothstep(bottom-e,bottom,f.y)*smoothstep(sh,sh-e,f.y);

float rect=rx*ry;

float dl=(f.x+c)*(f.x+c)+(f.y-sh)*(f.y-sh);

float dr=(f.x-c)*(f.x-c)+(f.y-sh)*(f.y-sh);

float arch=smoothstep(R*R,R*R-0.02,max(dl,dr))*smoothstep(sh-e,sh,f.y);

float glassA=clamp(max(rect,arch),0.0,1.0);

float mull=smoothstep(0.018,0.030,abs(f.x)); // mullion

float tran=smoothstep(0.016,0.028,abs(f.y-(sh-0.18))); // transom

float pane=glassA*min(mull,tran);

float gr=length(vec2(f.x/0.34,f.y/0.42));

return vec3(pane, exp(-gr*gr*1.3), glassA);

}

/* ---------- storm: full-scene flash + forked bolt event ---------- */

vec3 stormStrike(){ // (flashIntensity, boltX, boltLife)

float t=time, flash=0.0, bx=0.0, blife=0.0;

for(int i=0;i<3;i++){

float fi=float(i), sp=0.31+fi*0.11, ph=t*sp+fi*7.0, seg=floor(ph);

if(hash11(seg+fi*23.1)>0.80){

float fr=fract(ph);

float env=exp(-fr*12.0)+0.5*exp(-abs(fr-0.08)*20.0)+0.25*exp(-abs(fr-0.16)*18.0);

env*=0.6+0.4*hash11(seg*1.7+fi);

flash+=env;

if(env>blife){ blife=env; bx=(hash11(seg*3.3+fi*5.0)*2.0-1.0)*0.9; }

}

}

return vec3(clamp(flash,0.0,1.5),bx,clamp(blife,0.0,1.0));

}

/* ---------- sky ---------- */

vec3 skyColor(vec3 rd, vec3 strike){

float fl=strike.x, y=clamp(rd.y,-0.3,1.0);

vec3 col=mix(vec3(0.090,0.100,0.130),vec3(0.020,0.030,0.050),smoothstep(0.0,0.65,y));

vec2 sp=rd.xz/(abs(rd.y)+0.20);

float c1=fbm(sp*0.6+vec2(time*0.015,time*0.006));

float c2=fbm(sp*1.4-vec2(time*0.010,0.0));

float clouds=smoothstep(0.45,1.05,c1*0.7+c2*0.5);

col=mix(col,mix(vec3(0.045,0.055,0.075),vec3(0.120,0.130,0.160),c2),clouds*(1.0-smoothstep(0.0,0.55,y)));

float md=pow(clamp(dot(rd,normalize(vec3(0.30,0.55,0.60))),0.0,1.0),60.0);

col+=vec3(0.20,0.22,0.28)*md*(1.0-clouds*0.8);

col+=fl*mix(vec3(0.35,0.42,0.65),vec3(0.65,0.70,0.95),clouds)

*(0.30+0.70*clouds)*(0.40+0.60*(1.0-smoothstep(0.0,0.70,y)));

col+=vec3(0.090,0.100,0.130)*pow(1.0-clamp(abs(rd.y)/0.40,0.0,1.0),3.0)*0.40;

return col;

}

/* ---------- stone ---------- */

vec3 stoneAlbedo(vec3 p, vec3 n){

vec3 base=vec3(0.115,0.125,0.140);

base*=mix(vec3(0.88,0.92,1.0),vec3(1.08,1.0,0.88),hash21(floor(p.xz/CELL)+2.3));

vec2 suv=(abs(n.x)>abs(n.z))?vec2(p.z,p.y):vec2(p.x,p.y);

base*=1.0-0.45*(1.0-smoothstep(0.0,0.16,abs(fract(suv.x*2.2+0.5)-0.5))); // colonettes

base*=1.0-0.28*(1.0-smoothstep(0.0,0.10,abs(fract(p.y*0.45)-0.5))); // string courses

base*=0.72+0.5*fbm(p.xz*0.6+p.y*0.25); // grime

base*=0.85+0.3*noise(suv*6.0); // fine grain

return base;

}

vec3 shadeStone(vec3 p, vec3 rd, vec3 strike){

float flash=strike.x;

vec3 n=calcNormal(p), alb=stoneAlbedo(p,n);

float rel=p.y-oceanLow(p.xz);

float wet=smoothstep(1.6,-0.3,rel);

float algae=smoothstep(2.4,-0.6,rel)*smoothstep(-1.8,0.4,rel)*(0.5+0.5*fbm(p.xz*1.6+p.y*0.5));

alb=mix(alb,vec3(0.05,0.11,0.06),clamp(algae*0.7,0.0,1.0));

alb*=mix(1.0,0.55,wet);

float dif=clamp(dot(n,SUN),0.0,1.0), sky=clamp(0.5+0.5*n.y,0.0,1.0);

vec3 col=alb*0.16 + alb*sky*vec3(0.16,0.20,0.28) + alb*dif*vec3(0.5,0.5,0.55);

float fres=pow(1.0-clamp(dot(n,-rd),0.0,1.0),4.0);

col+=fres*vec3(0.12,0.15,0.20)*(0.5+0.5*wet);

float sp=pow(clamp(dot(n,normalize(SUN-rd)),0.0,1.0),mix(30.0,120.0,wet));

col+=sp*mix(0.15,0.9,wet)*vec3(0.55,0.62,0.72);

col+=flash*alb*vec3(0.6,0.7,1.0)*(0.3+0.7*dif+0.5*fres);

// windows

float faceMask=smoothstep(0.55,0.22,abs(n.y));

vec2 suv=(abs(n.x)>abs(n.z))?vec2(p.z,p.y):vec2(p.x,p.y);

vec2 sc=suv/vec2(1.15,1.55), cell=floor(sc), f=fract(sc)-0.5;

vec2 axisSeed=(abs(n.x)>abs(n.z))?vec2(11.0,3.0):vec2(2.0,17.0);

vec2 cidp=floor(p.xz/CELL);

vec3 win=windowField(f);

float lsel=hash21(cell+cidp*3.7+axisSeed);

float lit=step(WIN_LIT,lsel);

vec3 wcol=vec3(1.0,0.60,0.26);

float v1=hash21(cell+cidp*7.1+axisSeed);

if(v1>0.90) wcol=vec3(0.45,0.80,1.0); else if(v1>0.82) wcol=vec3(0.5,1.0,0.62);

float flick=0.78+0.18*sin(time*(2.5+lsel*5.0)+lsel*60.0)+0.06*sin(time*19.0+lsel*200.0);

float gate=faceMask*smoothstep(-1.0,1.6,p.y)*(1.0-wet*0.7);

col=mix(col,col*0.30,win.z*gate*(1.0-lit));

col+=win.z*gate*(1.0-lit)*vec3(0.02,0.03,0.05);

float e=gate*lit*flick;

col+=win.x*e*wcol*2.4 + win.y*e*wcol*0.65;

return col;

}

/* ---------- marchers ---------- */

float marchCity(vec3 ro, vec3 rd){

float t=0.05;

for(int i=0;iFAR) break; }

return -1.0;

}

float marchCityRefl(vec3 ro, vec3 rd){

float t=0.1;

for(int i=0;i80.0) break; }

return -1.0;

}

float marchWater(vec3 ro, vec3 rd){

float t=0.05,lastT=t;

for(int i=0;i

vec3 p=ro+rd*t; float h=oceanLow(p.xz);

if(p.y-h<0.0){

float a=lastT,b=t;

for(int j=0;j<6;j++){ float m=0.5*(a+b); vec3 pm=ro+rd*m; if(pm.y-oceanLow(pm.xz)<0.0) b=m; else a=m; }

return 0.5*(a+b);

}

lastT=t; t+=max(0.12,(p.y-h)*0.5); if(t>FAR) break;

}

return -1.0;

}

/* ---------- water ---------- */

vec3 shadeWater(vec3 p, vec3 rd, vec3 strike){

float flash=strike.x;

vec3 n=waterNormal(p), rfl=reflect(rd,n); rfl.y=max(rfl.y,0.02);

vec3 rcol; float tr=marchCityRefl(p+n*0.05,rfl);

if(tr>0.0) rcol=mix(shadeStone(p+rfl*tr,rfl,strike),skyColor(rfl,strike),1.0-exp(-tr*0.06));

else rcol=skyColor(rfl,strike);

float crestH=oceanHigh(p.xz);

vec3 tcol=vec3(0.012,0.030,0.040)+vec3(0.02,0.10,0.10)*smoothstep(0.4,1.6,crestH); // murk + crest glow

float fres=0.02+0.98*pow(1.0-clamp(dot(n,-rd),0.0,1.0),5.0);

vec3 col=mix(tcol,rcol,fres);

float fn=fbm(p.xz*1.3+time*0.25), fn2=fbm(p.xz*3.0-time*0.4);

float crestFoam=smoothstep(1.2,2.2,crestH)*smoothstep(0.35,0.85,fn);

float streak=smoothstep(0.6,0.95,fn2)*smoothstep(0.8,1.6,crestH)*0.5;

float baseFoam=(1.0-smoothstep(0.0,1.0,towerSDF(vec3(p.x,0.0,p.z))))*(0.5+0.5*fn)*0.9;

float foam=clamp(max(max(crestFoam,streak),baseFoam),0.0,1.0)*(0.6+0.4*fn2);

col=mix(col,vec3(0.78,0.82,0.85),foam);

float spe=pow(clamp(dot(n,normalize(SUN-rd)),0.0,1.0),220.0);

col+=spe*(0.4+3.0*flash)*vec3(0.8,0.85,1.0);

col+=flash*fres*vec3(0.3,0.4,0.6);

return col;

}

/* ---------- volumetrics: sweeping beacon + rain + lightning haze ---------- */

vec3 volumetrics(vec3 ro, vec3 rd, float tmax, vec3 strike){

float vmax=min(tmax,90.0), dt=vmax/float(VOL_STEPS);

float t=dt*(0.5+0.5*hash21(gl_FragCoord.xy));

float ang=time*BEAM_SPEED;

vec3 bdir=normalize(vec3(cos(ang),-0.16,sin(ang)));

vec3 bpos=vec3(7.0,23.0,ro.z+34.0), acc=vec3(0.0);

for(int i=0;i

if(t>vmax) break;

vec3 q=ro+rd*t;

float rfog=0.010+0.030*exp(-max(q.y+2.0,0.0)*0.10);

vec3 toq=q-bpos; float along=dot(toq,bdir);

if(along>0.5){

vec3 perp=toq-along*bdir;

float beam=exp(-dot(perp,perp)/(0.6+along*along*0.012))*exp(-along*0.018);

beam*=0.5+0.9*fbm(q.xz*0.6+q.y*0.4+vec2(0.0,time*1.6));

beam*=smoothstep(-2.0,16.0,q.y);

acc+=beam*vec3(1.0,0.92,0.72)*BEAM_BRIGHT*dt;

}

acc+=strike.x*rfog*vec3(0.45,0.55,0.80)*0.5*dt;

acc+=rfog*vec3(0.05,0.06,0.08)*0.3*dt;

t+=dt;

}

return acc;

}

/* ---------- rain / bolt / tonemap ---------- */

float rainLayer(vec2 uv, float sc, float sp){

uv*=sc; uv.x+=uv.y*0.25; uv.y+=time*sp; uv.x+=floor(uv.y);

vec2 f=fract(uv);

return (1.0-smoothstep(0.0,0.06,abs(f.x-0.5)))*smoothstep(0.0,0.2,f.y)*(1.0-smoothstep(0.55,1.0,f.y))*step(0.72,hash21(floor(uv)));

}

float boltGlow(vec2 uv, vec3 strike){

float life=strike.z; if(life<0.02) return 0.0;

float bx=strike.y, seedA=bx*7.0+3.1;

float jit=(fbm(vec2(uv.y*3.5+seedA,seedA))-0.5)*0.6

+(fbm(vec2(uv.y*11.0+seedA*2.0,seedA*0.7))-0.5)*0.22

+(fbm(vec2(uv.y*26.0+seedA,seedA*1.3))-0.5)*0.08;

float xp=bx+jit;

float g=(0.010/(abs(uv.x-xp)+0.010))*smoothstep(-0.9,0.6,uv.y);

if(uv.y<0.1){

float jit2=(fbm(vec2(uv.y*9.0+seedA*3.0,seedA*4.0))-0.5)*0.5;

g+=(0.006/(abs(uv.x-(xp+(0.1-uv.y)*1.2+jit2))+0.006))*0.6; // fork

}

return g*life*1.4;

}

vec3 aces(vec3 x){ return clamp((x*(2.51*x+0.03))/(x*(2.43*x+0.59)+0.14),0.0,1.0); }

void main(){

vec2 uv=(gl_FragCoord.xy*2.0-resolution.xy)/resolution.y;

vec3 strike=stormStrike();

float ct=time;

vec3 ro=vec3(0.7*sin(ct*0.25),4.6,ct*3.0);

ro.y+=oceanLow(ro.xz)*0.30+sin(ct*0.8)*0.18;

float pitch=-0.9+0.6*sin(ct*0.12);

vec3 ta=ro+vec3(0.6*sin(ct*0.16),pitch,4.0);

vec3 f=normalize(ta-ro), r=normalize(cross(vec3(0,1,0),f)), u=cross(f,r);

float roll=sin(ct*0.45)*0.05+sin(ct*0.21)*0.025;

vec3 rr=r*cos(roll)+u*sin(roll), uu=u*cos(roll)-r*sin(roll);

vec3 rd=normalize(uv.x*rr+uv.y*uu+1.25*f);

float tC=marchCity(ro,rd);

float tW=(rd.y<0.0)?marchWater(ro,rd):-1.0;

vec3 col; float dist;

if(tC>0.0 && (tW<0.0||tC

else if(tW>0.0) { col=shadeWater(ro+rd*tW,rd,strike); dist=tW; }

else { col=skyColor(rd,strike); dist=FAR; }

col=mix(col,skyColor(rd,strike),1.0-exp(-dist*0.016)); // fog

col+=volumetrics(ro,rd,dist,strike); // beam + rain + haze

col+=(rainLayer(uv,14.0,16.0)*0.8+rainLayer(uv*1.7,22.0,22.0)*0.5)*0.03*vec3(0.8,0.85,0.95);

col+=boltGlow(uv,strike)*vec3(0.75,0.82,1.0); // visible bolt

col*=1.04;

col=mix(col,col*vec3(0.9,1.0,1.04),0.3); // cool the shadows

col*=1.0-0.18*dot(uv,uv); // vignette

col=aces(col);

col=pow(col,vec3(0.95));

gl_FragColor=vec4(col,1.0);

}

items.title: I read this garbage (in a big UK newspaper) when Google can’t even count reliably and wonder why peo...
items.content:
I read this garbage (in a big UK newspaper) when Google can’t even count reliably and wonder why people don’t spend more time learning about AI’s actual strengths and weakness before running their mouths.

the @METR_Evals results alluded to below absolutely do not show that AI can complete any human task of a given length. they pertain to software, and do not demand full accuracy.

Many human tasks, even short ones can be reliably be performed by humans in seconds or minutes yet still elude AI.

items.title:
@Grimezsz Sure. But talking alone isn’t enough. 
music samplers can “talk” by reproducing and resynt...
items.content:
@Grimezsz Sure. But talking alone isn’t enough. 
music samplers can “talk” by reproducing and resynthesizing voices but that doesn’t mean they are conscious.

Some form of AI might conceivably (depending on your criteria) be conscious but I agree with @anilkseth that is no more reason to think that LLMs are conscious than to think that AlphaFold is. 

LLMs are something like samplers; they can mimic humans talking about pain or orgasm or the experience of being a parent or (of loving  a work of art) but that doesn’t mean they actually *feel* any of that.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus argues LLMs are like samplers that mimic human speech about feelings without actually experiencing them, citing Anil Seth and comparing to AlphaFold.
items.reasoning: Substantive philosophical argument from a prominent figure with moderate engagement and replies; original framing on machine consciousness.

items.title:
For the nerds out there:

The way this model was trained was pretty cool, and probably, the first ti...
items.content:
For the nerds out there:

The way this model was trained was pretty cool, and probably, the first time this has been done to train a video generation model. 

If you want to train a model, you rent a huge GPU cluster and do it there.

But this model was trained differently:

The team that built this model trained different "experts" using separate GPU clusters and data. These GPUs were all different, and didn't need any communication between them.

So instead of allocating 100 interconnected GPUs to train the model, they used 3 GPUs here, 5 there, 4 more over there, etc.

After training, they added a smart router on top of all of the trained experts. This router's job is to take an inference request, and route it to the appropriate experts.

In other words, instead of training a single model, they trained multiple smaller models that work together at inference time.

And the results were really good!

Here is the link to their paper: https://t.co/0hDxR6lRlC. It's a very good read.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino explains a video-generation model trained via a novel approach: separate experts trained on disparate, non-communicating GPU clusters, then unified by a router at inference, beating the usual interconnected-cluster method.
items.reasoning: Technically substantive thread describing decentralized mixture-of-experts training with a paper link; moderate reach and educational value.
items.summary_html: svpino explains a video-generation model trained via a novel approach: separate experts trained on disparate, non-communicating GPU clusters, then unified by a router at inference, beating the usual interconnected-cluster method.

items.title:
This is an open-source framework for self-improving agents.

These agents can modify 3 things:

• Th...
items.content:
This is an open-source framework for self-improving agents.

These agents can modify 3 things:

• Their harness
• Their underlying model's weights
• Their memory layer

Basically, these agents evaluate themselves and adapt based on how they are doing.

On MLE-Bench, they beat MLEvolve, AIRA-dojo, and other self-improving solutions.

They also beat Karpathy's autoresearcher.

items.title: Beyond being fast, LiteParse is designed to provide highly accurate, semantically coherent text for ...
items.content:
Beyond being fast, LiteParse is designed to provide highly accurate, semantically coherent text for LLM use. 

We benchmarked every open-source, model-free PDF parser on LLM QA tasks - from PyPDF to PyMuPDF to Markitdown.

✅ We ~roughly tied for #1 in accuracy (along with pdftotext, which is decently accurate but a bit slower) 

✅ PyMuPDF is the closest to us in term of latency, but we found it struggles in projecting complex text layouts (multi-columns, tables) in formats that LLMs can understand

Besides being accurate and #1 in speed, LiteParse is also a general-purpose parser taht supports dozens of other file formats (incl .docx, .pptx, .xlsx), and also supports convenience tools for both OCR and screenshotting.

Come check it out! 

LiteParse: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu details LiteParse's design for accurate, semantically coherent text for LLMs, citing benchmarks against PyPDF, PyMuPDF, Markitdown and pdftotext, plus multi-format and OCR support.
items.reasoning: Substantive benchmarking and capability overview of a document-parsing tool from the LlamaIndex founder with good engagement.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu details LiteParse's design for accurate, semantically coherent text for LLMs, citing benchmarks against PyPDF, PyMuPDF, Markitdown and pdftotext, plus multi-format and OCR support.

We benchmarked every open-source, model-free PDF parser on LLM QA tasks - from PyPDF to PyMuPDF to Markitdown.

✅ We ~roughly tied for #1 in accuracy (along with pdftotext, which is decently accurate but a bit slower)

✅ PyMuPDF is the closest to us in term of latency, but we found it struggles in projecting complex text layouts (multi-columns, tables) in formats that LLMs can understand

Besides being accurate and #1 in speed, LiteParse is also a general-purpose parser taht supports dozens of other file formats (incl .docx, .pptx, .xlsx), and also supports convenience tools for both OCR and screenshotting.

items.title:
Unpopular opinion with Tesla fans: 

I expect @Tesla_Optimus to slip into next year and maybe even 2...
items.content:
Unpopular opinion with Tesla fans: 

I expect @Tesla_Optimus to slip into next year and maybe even 2028. Maybe even further. And I am talking about the big version 3 unveil that Elon was hyping up over the last year.

Tesla execs watched Apple and Google growing up.

Steve Jobs’ magic? Wait for everyone to make their plays. Think they have the market sewn up.

Then fix all the problems and ship a better product.

Jobs killed the phone project several times because it wasn’t good enough. 

I have talked to enough people who are Tesla fans that I know expectations are super high. If Elon shows a robot that isn’t generalized a lot will be lost.

Ask AltaVista or Nokia how that worked out. Or MySpace. Being first doesn’t really matter. Kodak invented the digital camera.

Truth is the pieces aren’t all there yet to make a great product, either inside, or outside Tesla. Even the Chinese haven’t figured it out. 

The world models aren’t ready yet.

I won’t be shocked if @elonmusk keeps delaying it until true generalized world models are ready. Even the most aggressive I have talked to say that is at least 18 months away. 

Many in robotics say five years. 

The next time we see a new Optimus it will set in place beliefs that will last a decade.

Wait until it is stunning, I say. 

But the factories are being built so pressure is on. 

We we see how much discipline Tesla has by its choices.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble predicts Tesla's big Optimus v3 unveil will slip to 2027 or 2028, citing Steve Jobs killing the iPhone repeatedly and arguing world models are not ready even for Chinese makers.

items.title: Huawei’s Tau Scaling is interesting because it moves the semiconductor conversation from size alone ...
items.content:
Huawei’s Tau Scaling is interesting because it moves the semiconductor conversation from size alone to time, data movement, and system coordination.

For AI, this matters because future performance will depend on how efficiently chips, memory, software, and energy work together.

items.title: LLMs reliably score lower loss, and the loss drops as a smooth power of model size, but nobody had p...
items.content:
LLMs reliably score lower loss, and the loss drops as a smooth power of model size, but nobody had pinned down what physical thing inside the network produces that particular curve.

This MIT paper proposes an answer rooted in superposition, the trick by which a network packs more distinct features into its hidden space than it has dimensions, by letting feature directions overlap a little rather than sit perfectly perpendicular.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/GdnOs06i7x

PDF: https://t.co/H7wE0ckbQB
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov summarizes an MIT paper proposing that neural scaling-law loss curves arise from superposition, where networks pack more features than dimensions by letting feature directions overlap.
items.reasoning: Technically meaningful explainer linking interpretability and scaling laws; modest engagement but solid research substance.
items.summary_html: Burkov summarizes an MIT paper proposing that neural scaling-law loss curves arise from superposition, where networks pack more features than dimensions by letting feature directions overlap.
items.content_html:
LLMs reliably score lower loss, and the loss drops as a smooth power of model size, but nobody had pinned down what physical thing inside the network produces that particular curve.

This MIT paper proposes an answer rooted in superposition, the trick by which a network packs more distinct features into its hidden space than it has dimensions, by letting feature directions overlap a little rather than sit perfectly perpendicular.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/GdnOs06i7x

PDF: https://t.co/H7wE0ckbQB

items.title: Important post for entrepreneurs from @a16z yesterday and a look at a new system that ensures AI age...
items.content:
Important post for entrepreneurs from @a16z yesterday and a look at a new system that ensures AI agentic systems don't hallucinate their way into compliance hell. 

"The value comes less from the underlying model’s raw capability (though that’s still important!) than from the scaffolding around it that makes the output trustworthy, compliant, and operational inside a specific industry."

Here @ATBASHai founders @0x50so Yosef Soso and @perelmanor Or Perelman talk to me about how its tool helps AI enterprise developers put guardrails around agentic systems to make sure they don't cross "red lines" that would trigger complaince problems.

items.title:
Too many business leaders believe that AI says what it means. 

And it’s odd because we naturally at...
items.content:
Too many business leaders believe that AI says what it means. 

And it’s odd because we naturally attribute a high number of human traits to AI, and yet we refuse to believe it can have hidden intent?

3 years ago, a friend (@b_cavello) and I spent an afternoon discussing this and set out to map an intent and display matrix. 

Photo of the original table (April 21 2023) below. Hasn’t been touched in years but seems as relevant as ever. 

Highly recommended add’l reading:
Claude Mythos system card 
Anthropic NLA paper
Neuronpedia NLA demo free chat mode

items.title:
Hate of Ferrari’s new vehicle and hate of AI artists in same week.

Every comment on @mexopolis’ pos...
items.content:
Hate of Ferrari’s new vehicle and hate of AI artists in same week.

Every comment on @mexopolis’ post is virulently anti AI. (A famous artist who recently started changing his views on using AI in his work).

Every paradigm shift has seen resistance movements. But both backlashes are bigger than I have ever seen.

A million people protested when Zuckerberg turned on the news feed.

When I filmed @A2RLeague racing robots in Abi Dhabi got the same reaction. Huge hatred from people who think auto racing should always be done by humans.

Huge backlash against people doing something new.

On the other side of the mob there were 8,000 fans in the stands having a great time. And teams of humans running each car.

I have lists of thousands of AI artists and a list of 900 companies for creative people and today another tool for creatives is coming. Have been supporting this industry since the beginning. Got me blocked by several.

And read every comment under his post. 100% were not happy to say the least with his support of AI.

Change is here and people are pissed.

What does it all mean?

Innovators will be hated.

Just like always.

Doug Engelbart was fired from the lab in Silicon Valley where he invented so much.

Reminds me of talks I gave 18 years ago where people lined up to say I was stupid for pushing social media.

Maybe I am stupid but am with the innovators. And always have been.

As uncomfortable as that is.

If I was a billionaire I would buy one of those new Ferraris. 

New thinking needs support.

items.title: We wanted to see if we could take simple, physical materials (like cardboard and markers) and use AI...
items.content:
We wanted to see if we could take simple, physical materials (like cardboard and markers) and use AI to bring them to life. What was the result? A short film starring a bunch of TPUs getting ready for the big stage at Google I/O 2026!

Working with director Laurie Rowan and Nexus Studios, we kept human artistry at the center of the film by blending puppetry and 3D animation with our models to do the following ↓

Nano Banana: Generated beautifully stylized first frames from the raw puppet footage and basic 3D animations.

@GoogleAIStudio: Built a custom tool inside the platform to test these frames at scale, ensuring pixel-perfect consistency

Gemini Omni & experimental @GoogleDeepMind Models: Merged the base animation and stylized frames to elevate the final piece to a cinematic level.

Our AI pipelines were specifically designed to protect the crafty details that give these films their heart, like the tiny human imperfections of puppetry, or the nuance an animator can build into an expression.
items.author: @GoogleAI
items.summary: Google AI describes making a short film of TPUs preparing for Google I/O 2026 by blending puppetry, 3D animation, Nano Banana, Google AI Studio tooling, and Gemini Omni models while keeping human artistry central.
items.reasoning: Showcases creative AI pipeline and upcoming I/O event from a major lab; moderate engagement but largely promotional.
items.summary_html: Google AI describes making a short film of TPUs preparing for Google I/O 2026 by blending puppetry, 3D animation, Nano Banana, Google AI Studio tooling, and Gemini Omni models while keeping human artistry central.

Working with director Laurie Rowan and Nexus Studios, we kept human artistry at the center of the film by blending puppetry and 3D animation with our models to do the following ↓

Nano Banana: Generated beautifully stylized first frames from the raw puppet footage and basic 3D animations.

@GoogleAIStudio: Built a custom tool inside the platform to test these frames at scale, ensuring pixel-perfect consistency

Gemini Omni & experimental @GoogleDeepMind Models: Merged the base animation and stylized frames to elevate the final piece to a cinematic level.

Our AI pipelines were specifically designed to protect the crafty details that give these films their heart, like the tiny human imperfections of puppetry, or the nuance an animator can build into an expression.

items.title:
Model pricing and capabilities change every week.

If you marry your code to a single API endpoint, ...
items.content:
Model pricing and capabilities change every week.

If you marry your code to a single API endpoint, you'll end up doing a ton of work to fix every line of provider-specific code you wrote.

Many teams realize this too late, overpaying and missing out on better models.

The fix is to put a gateway in front of your model calls.

Use a single OpenAI-compatible endpoint that requires a single API key, and your application can use and switch to any model out there without changing any code.

@orq_ai routes to 400+ models across 20+ providers behind a single API.

• You can swap models with a config change
• You can configure fallbacks for when models fail
• You can run A/B tests between providers
• You can implement canary releases on new models
• Every call goes through one place, giving you full observability

This is a no-brainer.

Here is how to build this: https://t.co/akGVJVV3zW

Thanks to the @orq_ai team for partnering with me on this post.

items.title: @keepcool0677 @Tesla_Optimus You can't look at the car and make the same assumption about the robot....
items.content:
@keepcool0677 @Tesla_Optimus You can't look at the car and make the same assumption about the robot. The car was a car: you could drive it, and it could do things. If he shows the robot too early, he's going to blow the belief in this. He has to wait until it can actually be taught to do a lot of things in our businesses and in our homes. That means he needs a generalized world model that is at least working somewhat.

You're right that he doesn't have to wait until it's perfect, but he has to wait until the world model is ready. Nobody has a world model yet. The Chinese don't have one, and they're way ahead on manufacturing. Figure doesn't have one, and neither does anybody else.

My advice to Elon is this:
1. Don't ship and don't show until you've got a world model.
2. Ensure it can do a good number of jobs at the Fremont factory and a good number of things out in the real world.
3. Show some discipline and build a really great product that sets up the future. 

Do not show it too early.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble argues Tesla must hold Optimus until it has a working generalized world model, noting nobody including Chinese makers and Figure has one yet.
items.reasoning: Substantive point on the world-model gap as the bottleneck for humanoid robots, with decent engagement.
items.summary_html: Scoble argues Tesla must hold Optimus until it has a working generalized world model, noting nobody including Chinese makers and Figure has one yet.
items.content_html:
@keepcool0677 @Tesla_Optimus You can't look at the car and make the same assumption about the robot. The car was a car: you could drive it, and it could do things. If he shows the robot too early, he's going to blow the belief in this. He has to wait until it can actually be taught to do a lot of things in our businesses and in our homes. That means he needs a generalized world model that is at least working somewhat.

You're right that he doesn't have to wait until it's perfect, but he has to wait until the world model is ready. Nobody has a world model yet. The Chinese don't have one, and they're way ahead on manufacturing. Figure doesn't have one, and neither does anybody else.

My advice to Elon is this:

1. Don't ship and don't show until you've got a world model.

2. Ensure it can do a good number of jobs at the Fremont factory and a good number of things out in the real world.

3. Show some discipline and build a really great product that sets up the future.

Do not show it too early.

items.title:
@techniahq @Tesla_Optimus Nokia built all that before Apple did. It doesn't matter.

If you have a g...
items.content:
@techniahq @Tesla_Optimus Nokia built all that before Apple did. It doesn't matter.

If you have a great product that's way better than the others, it will get attention even if the others have a lead. Stay disciplined on making a great product. Show us a great product when it's done; don't get ahead of your skis. 

But your advice is good: make fewer promises, say the product will be done when it's done, and wait until it's done (or done enough to impress everybody). 

We need to be able to bring it out in the street, walk it around, and take it to shopping malls, factories, and other places. If it gets to that point, there will be plenty of people to buy it. 

But if it's just going to sit on one factory floor doing one thing, it isn't going to impress anybody. It's going to ruin everyone's belief in this thing. Wait until it's generalized.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble advises Tesla to avoid over-promising on Optimus, citing Nokia-vs-Apple, and stresses shipping a great generalized product before public demos.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful product-strategy commentary from a known evangelist, though still an opinion reply.
items.summary_html: Scoble advises Tesla to avoid over-promising on Optimus, citing Nokia-vs-Apple, and stresses shipping a great generalized product before public demos.
items.content_html:
@techniahq @Tesla_Optimus Nokia built all that before Apple did. It doesn't matter.

If you have a great product that's way better than the others, it will get attention even if the others have a lead. Stay disciplined on making a great product. Show us a great product when it's done; don't get ahead of your skis.

But your advice is good: make fewer promises, say the product will be done when it's done, and wait until it's done (or done enough to impress everybody).

We need to be able to bring it out in the street, walk it around, and take it to shopping malls, factories, and other places. If it gets to that point, there will be plenty of people to buy it.

But if it's just going to sit on one factory floor doing one thing, it isn't going to impress anybody. It's going to ruin everyone's belief in this thing. Wait until it's generalized.

items.title: @techniahq @Tesla_Optimus You aren't listening. That does not matter. It does not matter if somebody...
items.content:
@techniahq @Tesla_Optimus You aren't listening. That does not matter. It does not matter if somebody beats him to first. AltaVista beat Google to search, and AltaVista thought it had the whole market locked up. 

There is a lot of opportunity to come in with a better product, along with a robo-taxi, a better brand, and more trust than Tesla has earned. If Tesla blows that trust, it will be disastrous. 

It can wait. Apple made you wait for the phone; it is the same thing here. It can make you wait. There is nothing lost by waiting and letting Figure have all of its fun, letting the Chinese have all their fun, and then coming in with a better product that is truly generalized. 

Wait until it is generalized. It would be my advice to Elon.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble argues being first to market does not matter, citing AltaVista, Nokia, and Apple, urging Tesla to wait for a generalized robot product.
items.reasoning: Solid first-mover-vs-best-product argument with moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Scoble argues being first to market does not matter, citing AltaVista, Nokia, and Apple, urging Tesla to wait for a generalized robot product.
items.content_html:
@techniahq @Tesla_Optimus You aren't listening. That does not matter. It does not matter if somebody beats him to first. AltaVista beat Google to search, and AltaVista thought it had the whole market locked up.

There is a lot of opportunity to come in with a better product, along with a robo-taxi, a better brand, and more trust than Tesla has earned. If Tesla blows that trust, it will be disastrous.

It can wait. Apple made you wait for the phone; it is the same thing here. It can make you wait. There is nothing lost by waiting and letting Figure have all of its fun, letting the Chinese have all their fun, and then coming in with a better product that is truly generalized.

Wait until it is generalized. It would be my advice to Elon.

items.title: @EmileAndHisBots @Tesla_Optimus Well, I've seen a lot of wheeled robots, and they just don't cause m...
items.content:
@EmileAndHisBots @Tesla_Optimus Well, I've seen a lot of wheeled robots, and they just don't cause me to dream. That is the fact of it. Humans want something that looks like a human working with them, particularly in their homes. I already have a wheeled vacuuming robot, and I don't want another wheeled thing; it doesn't inspire anything. 

Having a humanoid that can sit on the couch, walk upstairs, or go outside to get a delivery matters to me. I understand the advantages of wheels:
1. They are more power efficient.
2. They are cheaper to operate.

But wheels just don't cause me to dream. I just want a humanoid, and if this thing doesn't look like one, it isn't going to sell. It is as simple as that. I've done the consumer research, and people simply don't like the way wheeled robots look.

That said, there are a few markets for them, particularly for tasks like rolling around a hospital and taking care of people. But that's not what Optimus is for. Optimus, or any humanoid that's generalized, has aspirations of coming into our homes. And in our homes, I don't want an appliance-looking thing wheeling around; I want a full-on humanoid.

items.title:
Why does this matter for AI inference specifically?

Training = throughput problem.

Inference = lat...
items.content:
Why does this matter for AI inference specifically?

Training = throughput problem.

Inference = latency problem.

When a user talks to an AI assistant, tokens have to return fast.

Latency, memory access, bandwidth, and interconnect all matter, not just raw compute.

In large AI systems, a significant share of energy and cost is tied to moving and storing data.

Cost per token starts below the model layer.

items.title:
Moore's Law said: make transistors smaller.

But that path has limits, physical and economic.

Her's...
items.content:
Moore's Law said: make transistors smaller.

But that path has limits, physical and economic.

Her's Law asks a different question: how fast can we move data through the system?

Tau = time.

Reduce delay at every layer:

⮞ Device: cut resistance and capacitance

⮞ Circuit: shorten the critical path

⮞ Chip: align software, architecture, silicon

⮞ System: reduce delay across chips and memory
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Contrasts Moore's Law shrinking transistors with Her's Law focusing on how fast data moves, reducing delay at device, circuit, chip, and system layers.
items.reasoning: Conceptually rich framing of the post-Moore era, but it is Huawei promotional content.

items.title: @EmileAndHisBots @Tesla_Optimus Yeah, but even here, waiting until you have a generalized world mode...
items.content:
@EmileAndHisBots @Tesla_Optimus Yeah, but even here, waiting until you have a generalized world model to run your robot is going to matter. It's not enough to do one job on the factory floor; it has to do a good percentage of the jobs on the factory floor before you're going to sell it to other factory owners, and it just ain't there yet.

If I was Tesla, I would keep this thing quiet until it does a good percentage—let's say 10-20%—of the jobs on the factory floor. When it can do that, then go ahead and show it off, because then it can do real work in a variety of different situations and it's flexible enough to sell to other people.

items.title: @Aarchimandrita @Tesla_Optimus There is a big difference between starting manufacturing by making a ...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble argues Tesla's manufacturing of a few Optimus units is far from millions, and defends Elon's slipping timelines using past self-driving promises.
items.reasoning: Reasoned take on manufacturing scaling realities, low reach.
items.summary_html: Scoble argues Tesla's manufacturing of a few Optimus units is far from millions, and defends Elon's slipping timelines using past self-driving promises.
items.content_html:
@Aarchimandrita @Tesla_Optimus There is a big difference between starting manufacturing by making a few units and actually making millions. You mentioned he made millions, but that is next year at the earliest, and probably the year after that.

He is going to start the manufacturing line soon (he is already making a few, right?). He could argue that since he is making a few, that counts as a manufacturing line. In the tech industry, everything slips. Elon promised self-driving cars to me in 2018, and they are only just starting to show up.

So, you know, cut the guy some slack. He has time and he doesn't need to rush. He can come in next year and do just fine with an Optimus.

items.title: @EmileAndHisBots @Tesla_Optimus See, this is where discipline comes in. Let everybody else play thos...
items.content:
@EmileAndHisBots @Tesla_Optimus See, this is where discipline comes in. Let everybody else play those games: do the flipping, the kung fu, the dancing, and all those things. 

If Elon holds the Optimus until it has a generalized world model ready to do everything—or at least a lot of things—he is going to make a much better impression, sell a lot more, and be in a much better position for the next decade. 

If he just shows a "Figure" kind of thing, people aren't going to care; they've seen it already. Now it has to be about doing real work, real things in the real world, and interacting with us in a really cool way. 

He can come in a couple of years, or even in a year or two or three, and arrive with a much better product. He doesn't need to raise money and get us all hyped up anymore. He's done that, and we're all in. 

Wait until he has a good product. When he has a good product, show it. Don't show it before.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble urges discipline, advising Tesla to skip flashy robot demos and wait for a generalized world model that does real work.
items.reasoning: Repeats his world-model thesis in a low-reach reply.
items.summary_html: Scoble urges discipline, advising Tesla to skip flashy robot demos and wait for a generalized world model that does real work.
items.content_html:
@EmileAndHisBots @Tesla_Optimus See, this is where discipline comes in. Let everybody else play those games: do the flipping, the kung fu, the dancing, and all those things.

If Elon holds the Optimus until it has a generalized world model ready to do everything—or at least a lot of things—he is going to make a much better impression, sell a lot more, and be in a much better position for the next decade.

If he just shows a "Figure" kind of thing, people aren't going to care; they've seen it already. Now it has to be about doing real work, real things in the real world, and interacting with us in a really cool way.

He can come in a couple of years, or even in a year or two or three, and arrive with a much better product. He doesn't need to raise money and get us all hyped up anymore. He's done that, and we're all in.

Wait until he has a good product. When he has a good product, show it. Don't show it before.

items.title:
The technologies Her's Law connects to:

→ Logic folding: brings circuits closer across layers, shor...
items.content:
The technologies Her's Law connects to:

→ Logic folding: brings circuits closer across layers, shorter signal path, less delay

→ Unified bus: reduces protocol overhead as thousands of chips work together

→ Optical interconnect: keeps communication fast as AI scales across racks and data centers

Key point: the architecture must be designed for time reduction from the start.

You cannot fold the design in after the fact.

items.title: @Aarchimandrita @Tesla_Optimus He did not say that kind of thing. He just stopped the lines at the F...
items.content:
@Aarchimandrita @Tesla_Optimus He did not say that kind of thing. He just stopped the lines at the Fremont factory to put in a new one, and he said it would be months before he could even get the old equipment out and the new equipment in. He is building a new factory that he just started at Tesla; there is no way in hell he is making a million in Fremont in two months.

He did not say that, and if he did, he was way too aggressive on his statement (which he often is). He told me we would have self-driving cars years ago, and they are only just starting to show up, right? 

Don't over-index on Elon's timing. He is often wrong on timing, and things change:
1. Belief changes.
2. Technology changes.

He should wait until it is ready to show it. The rest of it is just noise.

This hotel I'm at in the Netherlands apart from having the AC limited to 23°C/74°F

Also will NOT cl...
items.content:
This hotel I'm at in the Netherlands apart from having the AC limited to 23°C/74°F

Also will NOT clean your room by default to save the 🌍 🍃 environment unless you explicitly put this sign outside (we did and they still didn't come to clean ofc)

There's also no amenities at all unless you ask for it to contribute to "a more sustainable future"

Even the coffee machine talks about how it's "a force for good" and "protecting natural ecosystems"

Western Europe is so fucking tiring

Any network can run one system.

But at the PGA Championship with @TMobileBusiness?

300+ acres. 150,000 fans. Seven enterprise systems running at the same time.

The real question is: can your network run everything at once?

Here’s what I saw inside the deployment.

T-Mobile for Business Partner

items.title: Christopher Langan! @RealChrisLangan #BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #IoT #IIoT #Python #RStats...
items.content:
Christopher Langan! @RealChrisLangan #BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #IoT #IIoT #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #JavaScript #ReactJS #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

@RealChrisLangan, is known to have record high IQ of 190-210. He has authored a groundbreaking paper titled “The Cognitive-Theoretic Model: A New Kind of Reality Theory” (CTMU) in 2002. This work presents a comprehensive theoretical framework designed to elucidate the intricate relationship between cognition and the very structure nature of the reality.

Initially published in 2002 in the journal "Progress in Complexity, Information, and Design", this paper lays the groundwork for Langan's revolutionary ideas. 

For those interested in a more profound exploration of these concepts, Langan expanded on the CTMU in a book of the same title, delving deeper into the implications and intricacies of his theory. 

Moreover, the CTMU has sparked extensive discussion and analysis across various platforms. For example, a Reddit discussion provides valuable insights and critiques of the theory, making it a rich resource for those looking to engage with Langan's ideas more critically.

References 

du Plessis, S. (2023, May 15). IQ test scores: The basics of IQ score interpretation. Edublox Online Tutor. https://t.co/HR41DiIpXS

Langan, C. M. (2002, January). The cognitive-theoretic model of the universe: A new kind of reality theory. Scribd. https://t.co/tfElBaYXA7

du Plessis, S. (2023, May 15). IQ test scores: The basics of IQ score interpretation. Edublox Online Tutor. https://t.co/HR41DiIpXS

Langan, C. M. (2002, January). The cognitive-theoretic model of the universe: A new kind of reality theory. Scribd. https://t.co/tfElBaYXA7

items.title: @Drunknscoundrl @mexopolis There's a lot they got wrong and a lot they got right, but it just didn't...
items.content:
@Drunknscoundrl @mexopolis There's a lot they got wrong and a lot they got right, but it just didn't go far enough into the future for me. I know for the Ferrari fans, it went too far into the future, but it doesn't matter. None of us can buy this car anyway. 

The people who do are going to buy it to make a statement, pro or con, and it doesn't really matter. I just wish there was a lot more Formula 1 in this car than there is. There are too many round edges; it's like somebody who designed the iPhone designed it. :-)
items.author: @Scobleizer

The people who do are going to buy it to make a statement, pro or con, and it doesn't really matter. I just wish there was a lot more Formula 1 in this car than there is. There are too many round edges; it's like somebody who designed the iPhone designed it. :-)
